problem.title: Difficulty Distance
problem.description: Mimi, a rhythm game released in 201x, has been attracting more and more players - from young teenagers to adults. As a social-based game, it requires players to join in pairs and choose the same song to play together (note that in this fictional version of the game, each song only has only one level of difficulty). At a certain arcade, there is a club of n Mimi players who are about to participate in a practice session. The i-th player has a preferred song difficulty level a_i, which also represents their skill level. No two players have the same skill level. The team leader wants to select a subset of players and organize them into pairs such that the difference in skill levels between the two players in each pair is at most 1. Formally, suppose that x0(i), x1(i) are the indices of the players in the i-th pair; then it must hold that a_{x0(i)} - a_{x1(i)} ≤ 1. The team leader aims to form as many pairs of players as possible for the practice session. Your task is to count the number of ways to form the maximum number of pairs. Two ways are considered different iff there exists at least one pair of players that appear in one way but not in the other.
problem.input_format:
The first line contains the number of test cases τ (1 ≤ τ ≤ 2·10^5). For each test case:
- The first line contains an integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 2·10^5) — the number of players in the club.
- The second line contains n integers a1, a2, ..., an (1 ≤ ai ≤ 10^9) — the skill level of the players. All skill levels are distinct.
The sum of n over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5.
problem.output_format: For each test case, output a single integer — the number of ways to form the maximum number of pairs, modulo 998244353.
constraints: Sum of n over all test cases ≤ 2·10^5
problem.tle_strategy: n=max with adversarial intervals/tasks and equal keys

all_testcases.description: Edge case with minimum n=1 (single player) and a small normal case where two consecutive skill levels allow exactly one pair.
Mixed case: first test has unsorted distinct skills requiring optimal pairing, second test forms maximum pairs with a chain of consecutive values.
Edge case with minimum n=1, only one player, no pairs possible
All skill levels consecutive, maximum pairing possible with many ways
No two players within distance 1, only zero pairs possible
Mixed gaps and consecutive blocks to test greedy/DP handling
KILLER - sorted ascending, moderate size to stress greedy DP
